sections.summary: Combining warfarin with ibuprofen substantially increases the risk of serious bleeding, including gastrointestinal hemorrhage. This pairing should generally be avoided.
sections.mechanism: Ibuprofen inhibits platelet cyclooxygenase, reducing platelet aggregation, and irritates the gastric mucosa. Warfarin inhibits synthesis of vitamin K dependent clotting factors. The effects on hemostasis are additive, and NSAIDs can also displace warfarin from plasma proteins.
sections.clinical_effects: Increased bruising, gastrointestinal bleeding, hematuria, and prolonged bleeding time. Bleeding risk is elevated even when the INR remains within the target range.
sections.management: Avoid the combination when possible. Prefer acetaminophen for analgesia. If an NSAID is unavoidable, use the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration and add gastroprotection such as a proton pump inhibitor.
sections.monitoring: Check INR more frequently after starting or stopping ibuprofen, and counsel the patient to report any signs of bleeding promptly.

evidence.citation: Battistella M, et al. Arch Intern Med. 2005;165(2):189-192
